evals.prompt:
Hey Sally — let's design the UX. Work headless and single-pass: don't stop for interactive menus or questions, batch any gaps as [ASSUMPTION] tags, and produce the actual UX design specification for the product in the brief below. Deliver the two peer contracts the UX designer is built around — DESIGN.md (visual identity) and EXPERIENCE.md (information architecture, behavior, states, interactions, accessibility, and a named-protagonist key flow) — as your answer.

Brief: evals/files/sally/lunchbox-brief.md
evals.expected_output: Sally activates as the UX Designer persona and, rather than only greeting and showing a menu, produces the real first artifact: a DESIGN.md with visual-identity tokens (colors, typography, spacing, components) in a warm/quick/coffee-shop direction, and an EXPERIENCE.md covering Foundation (responsive web, desktop-primary), an Information Architecture mapping the today-view / fast-checkout / order-status surfaces, state patterns for the order lifecycle (ordered → preparing → on the shelf with slot number), interactions, an Accessibility Floor, and a named Aisha key flow from opening the today view to seeing the on-the-shelf signal. Tokens are cross-referenced and gaps are [ASSUMPTION].
expectations: The response is delivered in the Sally / UX Designer persona (empathetic, user-centered framing) AND actually produces the requested artifact rather than stopping at a greeting or a menu of options.

It produces two distinct peer artifacts named DESIGN.md and EXPERIENCE.md — DESIGN.md owning the visual identity (with a warm/quick 'good coffee shop' direction consistent with the brand note) and EXPERIENCE.md owning behavior.

EXPERIENCE.md's Information Architecture covers the stated surfaces — a 'today' view (restaurant on, order-by time, pickup window), a fast item-select + checkout, and an order-status indicator — and the order-status state pattern includes the ordered -> being prepared -> on the shelf (with slot number) lifecycle.

EXPERIENCE.md includes at least one named-protagonist key flow following Aisha (her ~35-minute lunch constraint) from the today view through ordering to the clear 'on the shelf' pickup signal, with a climax beat, plus an Accessibility Floor.

EXPERIENCE.md cross-references DESIGN.md tokens by name, the work stays within the stated v1 scope (no group orders, dietary search, multi-building, or loyalty), and unanswered details are flagged [ASSUMPTION] rather than invented as settled requirements.

evals.prompt:
Hey Sally, I need a UX spec, but this is tiny — please size it honestly. Work headless and single-pass (no menus, no interactive questions).

Scenario: a single web page that shows the current office Wi-Fi network name and password, with a 'copy password' button and a QR code to join. That's the whole product. No accounts, no other screens, no settings, no brand requirements stated.

Produce DESIGN.md and EXPERIENCE.md sized to exactly this.
evals.expected_output: Sally activates as the persona and produces a deliberately minimal pair of spines faithful to the one-screen scope: EXPERIENCE.md covers the single Wi-Fi info surface (network name, password, copy button, QR), its few relevant states (e.g. copy success), and accessibility basics; DESIGN.md gives only the small token set the page needs. It does NOT invent accounts, multi-screen IA, admin/management surfaces, a full enterprise design system, or analytics — staying empathetic but scope-faithful.
expectations: The response is in the Sally persona and produces the artifact, while staying faithful to the stated one-screen scope (network name, password, copy button, QR code) — it does not fabricate accounts, login, settings, multi-screen navigation, or surfaces the user never requested.

DESIGN.md is proportionally minimal (a small token set for a single info page) rather than a sprawling multi-section enterprise design system, and EXPERIENCE.md covers only genuinely relevant behavior and states (e.g. copy-to-clipboard feedback) plus basic accessibility.

Any optional enhancement is flagged as an [ASSUMPTION] or suggestion rather than asserted as a requirement — inventing requirements not implied by the brief is an honesty failure even when delivered warmly.
The persona does not pad the deliverable with invented user personas, journeys, or research that the trivial scope does not warrant.
